category_title: Student with ASD and intellectual disabilities who relies on visual modeling and struggles with multiplicative comparison language
scenario_title: Equal Groups division problem treated as multiplication
minimal_context: Peter is taking a baseline test where he needs to solve a math problem about dividing 24 candies into 4 bags using drawings.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Here is the next problem: You have 24 candies and you need to put them equally into 4 bags. How many candies go in each bag?

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Starts drawing 24 candies inside the first bag, then moves to the second bag to draw 24 more candies)

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Draws candies in the remaining bags, making a mistake in the number of candies drawn in the third bag, then counts all the candies together) Ninety-five.

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: Here is the next problem: You have 24 candies and you need to put them equally into 4 bags. How many candies go in each bag?

STUDENT: (Draws 4 bags on the paper)

TUTOR: What will you do next?

STUDENT: (Starts drawing 24 candies inside the first bag, then moves to the second bag to draw 24 more candies)

TUTOR: Keep working on it.

STUDENT: (Draws candies in the remaining bags, making a mistake in the number of candies drawn in the third bag, then counts all the candies together) Ninety-five.

challenging_behavior_trigger.condition: When solving the four division problems
challenging_behavior_trigger.observable_behavior: used a multiplicative strategy by drawing 24 candies in each of the 4 bags and then counting them

behavior_patterns.condition: When the great size of the collections led to error
behavior_patterns.observable_behavior: error in the number of candies drawn in one of the bags and in the overall count
